title: Hierarchical Document Trees
concept: Representing a document as nested sections (document > section > paragraph) for multi-level retrieval.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we learned about Parent-Child chunking. What is its main purpose?

options.text: Searching small child chunks but returning the larger parent chunk as context.
Combining all documents into a single massive chunk.
Using child chunks only and deleting the parent chunks entirely.

exercises.explain: Parent-child chunking indexes smaller, highly specific child chunks for search accuracy, but returns the larger parent chunk to the LLM to provide adequate context.

cards.title: Beyond Flat Text
cards.body: When you read a long book, you don't just see a continuous stream of words; you navigate using chapters, sections, and paragraphs. Why should our AI search engines treat documents like giant, flat text files when they have this beautiful built-in structure?

cards.title: Hierarchical Document Trees
cards.body: A Hierarchical Document Tree is a structured representation of a document that nests text segments by their natural layout, such as mapping an entire document to its main sections, and those sections to individual paragraphs. This allows a search engine to query at any level of detail.

cards.title: The Textbook Map
cards.body: Think of a hierarchical document tree like a nested outline of a textbook. You have the overall book, which contains chapters, which contain subheadings, which contain paragraphs. Instead of searching a flat pile of pages, you search this organized map.

cards.title: Structured Manuals
cards.body: In a user manual, a paragraph about 'Resetting the Wi-Fi' is nested under the 'Troubleshooting' section, which is nested under 'Product Manual'. If a user asks about Wi-Fi errors, the system retrieves the specific paragraph but also knows it belongs to 'Troubleshooting'.

cards.title: The Flat Chunking Trap
cards.body: Many believe that splitting documents into equal-sized 500-token chunks is always best. In reality, this cuts off headers and splits sentences, losing the document's logical flow. Organizing text hierarchically preserves the author's original structure.

cards.title: Flat vs. Hierarchical
cards.body: Flat chunking treats every piece of text as an isolated island, while hierarchical chunking preserves the natural relationships of the document outline.

left.text: Chunks are stored as an unstructured list with no knowledge of which section they came from.

right.text: Each paragraph is linked to its parent section, preserving context and structure.

exercises.prompt: How does a Hierarchical Document Tree organize text for retrieval?

options.text: It merges all documents in the database into one giant text block.
It structures documents into nested levels like document, section, and paragraph.
It deletes all headers and only keeps the footnotes.
It translates the text into multiple languages simultaneously.

exercises.explain: Hierarchical document trees represent documents at multiple levels of granularity (document, section, paragraph) rather than treating them as flat, independent pieces of text.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a document tree, you can search at the paragraph level but return the parent section to the LLM for better context.
exercises.explain: Yes, hierarchical retrieval allows the system to search small child paragraphs to find matches, but return the broader parent section to the LLM to provide richer context.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing concept about organizing documents for multi-level retrieval:
exercises.sentence: We can represent a document as nested sections to perform multi-level retrieval.
exercises.explain: Representing documents as nested sections (such as document > section > paragraph) allows the retriever to understand the context of where a piece of information resides.

exercises.prompt: Identify which of these data representations demonstrates a hierarchical document tree structure.

options.text:
- Paragraph 1: How to turn on the device.
- Paragraph 2: How to connect to bluetooth.
This document contains setup instructions. First, plug it in. Second, turn it on. Third, connect.
{ "document": "Setup Guide", "sections": [ { "title": "Hardware", "paragraphs": [ "Plug in the cable.", "Press the power button." ] } ] }

exercises.explain: Option C is generated by an AI assistant trying to create a hierarchical document tree JSON structure, showing a parent document with nested sections and child paragraphs. Options A and B are simple unstructured lists or plain text.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a complex RAG system for legal contracts. If you want the system to know exactly which clause a specific sentence belongs to, and which section that clause belongs to, what structure should you use?

options.text: A multi-level tree that links paragraphs to sections, and sections to the entire document.
A flat array of text chunks sorted alphabetically.
A database that only stores the very first sentence of every document.
A system that randomly groups sentences together to save space.

exercises.explain: Hierarchical document trees preserve the relationships between different levels of text. By storing parent-child-grandchild relationships, the system can retrieve a highly specific paragraph while also pulling in the context of its parent subsection or chapter to ensure the LLM gets the complete picture.
